Title: Nonaqueous fractionation and overexpression of fluorescent-tagged enzymes reveals the subcellular sites of L-theanine biosynthesis in tea

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: L-theanine formation from L-glutamate and ethylamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent condensation of L-glutamate with ethylamine to form L-theanine, analogous to glutamine synthetase activity with ammonia replaced by ethylamine.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step in shoot tissue, not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; nucleus (YFP signal detected in both cytosol and nucleus)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in green tissues, especially leaf
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest transcript levels in leaf tissue; reduced after long-term shading in shoots/leaves
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes importantly to tea functionality and quality
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tea young leaves and roots; in shoot cells mainly cytosol and chloroplast overall
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes L-theanine biosynthesis in tea shoot tissue
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana with ethylamine feeding; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS; qRT-PCR; YFP-based subcellular localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-term shading (30% full light for 2 weeks) decreased transcript abundance in shoots/leaves
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering elevated L-theanine in shoot tissues, potentially together with ethylamine supply-related genes; expression may be light sensitive
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Transcript decreases under shading despite increased shoot L-theanine, indicating environmental dependence and possible source-sink/translocation effects
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified as TS1 in earlier literature and renamed CsGS1.1 here; all five tested enzymes can synthesize L-theanine in vivo, but CsGS1.1 is inferred to be one key synthase in shoots based on expression plus localization
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsGS1.1 overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana increased L-theanine after ethylamine feeding, confirming in vivo synthetic capacity. 2. CsGS1.1 is highly expressed in green tissues, especially leaves, supporting a role in shoot L-theanine biosynthesis. 3. Cytosolic localization matches the major cytosolic L-theanine pool in tea shoots, making CsGS1.1 a key shoot L-theanine synthase.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: L-theanine formation from L-glutamate and ethylamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent condensation of L-glutamate with ethylamine to form L-theanine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in all tissues examined, highest in flower
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in root, stem, leaf and flower, highest in flower
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes importantly to tea functionality and quality
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Has in vivo L-theanine synthetic activity; likely contributes mainly in flowers rather than shoots/roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana with ethylamine feeding; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS; qRT-PCR; YFP-based subcellular localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Shading context discussed but no decisive tissue-specific key role shown under shade
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary enzyme for L-theanine engineering in non-leaf tissues, especially flowers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified as TS2 in earlier literature and renamed CsGS1.2 here; authors infer possible role in flower L-theanine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsGS1.2 overexpression increased L-theanine in Nicotiana after ethylamine feeding. 2. CsGS1.2 localizes to the cytosol. 3. Its highest expression in flowers suggests tissue-specific contribution to floral L-theanine biosynthesis rather than being a major shoot/root determinant.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: L-theanine formation from L-glutamate and ethylamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent condensation of L-glutamate with ethylamine to form L-theanine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in all tissues examined, highest in flower, but 10- to 100-fold lower than other GS transcripts
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low abundance in all tested tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes importantly to tea functionality and quality
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Has in vivo L-theanine synthetic activity but is unlikely to make a major contribution because of very low transcript abundance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana with ethylamine feeding; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS; qRT-PCR; YFP-based subcellular localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts with Mito-RFP colocalization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited immediate value as a native target due to low endogenous expression, but may be exploitable by ectopic expression
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely low native contribution despite catalytic competence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Additional GS homolog found in the tea genome; authors explicitly state it is unlikely to contribute significantly because of low abundance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsGS1.3 can synthesize L-theanine in vivo when overexpressed. 2. The protein localizes to mitochondria. 3. Native transcript abundance is much lower than other GS genes, so its contribution to endogenous L-theanine production is probably minor.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glutamine synthetase family (GS2-type) with predicted chloroplast transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Plastid/chloroplast-associated L-theanine formation from L-glutamate and ethylamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes ATP-dependent condensation of L-glutamate with ethylamine to form L-theanine; plastid-targeted GS2-type enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step for chloroplast-associated L-theanine synthesis in shoots
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplasts and mitochondria; chloroplast targeting depends on N-terminal transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in green tissues, especially leaf; very low in other tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf-specific/high in leaves; significantly down-regulated by long-term shading in shoots/leaves
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes importantly to tea functionality and quality
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tea young leaves and roots; in shoot cells substantial chloroplast pool under natural light
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplast-associated L-theanine biosynthesis/accumulation in tea shoots; reduced expression under shade is associated with loss of chloroplast L-theanine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana with ethylamine feeding; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS; qRT-PCR; YFP localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts; deletion analysis of N-terminal transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-term shading (30% full light for 2 weeks) strongly decreased CsGS2 expression and chloroplast distribution of L-theanine/L-glutamine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for enhancing shoot L-theanine, especially chloroplast-associated synthesis; light responsiveness should be considered in deployment
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Expression is environmentally sensitive; shade suppresses transcription and plastid contribution
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Dual-targeted protein, similar to Arabidopsis GLN2; authors identify CsGS2 as one of the key shoot L-theanine synthases and as explanatory factor for chloroplast L-theanine distribution
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsGS2 overexpression increases L-theanine production in Nicotiana after ethylamine feeding. 2. CsGS2 is preferentially expressed in leaves and localizes to chloroplasts and mitochondria, with chloroplast targeting mediated by the N terminus. 3. Reduced CsGS2 expression under shade correlates with a marked decline of chloroplast-localized L-theanine, establishing CsGS2 as a key shoot/plastid L-theanine synthase.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Root cytosolic L-theanine formation from L-glutamate and ethylamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions in vivo as an L-theanine-forming enzyme from L-glutamate and ethylamine in root cytosol.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step in root tissue
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed specifically in roots; very low in other tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-specific expression; transcript reduced after long-term shading in seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes importantly to tea functionality and quality
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tea roots and young leaves; in root cells almost entirely cytosolic
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes root cytosolic L-theanine biosynthesis and is the key synthase in roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana with ethylamine feeding; metabolite profiling by UPLC-QTOF-MS; qRT-PCR; YFP localization in Arabidopsis protoplasts; GFP localization in Arabidopsis hydroponic roots
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-term shading reduced transcript levels in seedlings, while root/stem L-theanine increased, implying substrate/translocation effects beyond transcript abundance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target to engineer higher root-derived L-theanine supply and possibly enhance translocation to shoots
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Transcript level alone may not predict metabolite accumulation under shade due to precursor availability and transport
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper concludes cytosol in tea root cells is the main site of L-theanine biosynthesis and that this occurs via CsTSI
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsTSI overexpression in Nicotiana benthamiana increased L-theanine after ethylamine feeding, confirming in vivo biosynthetic activity. 2. CsTSI is expressed specifically and strongly in roots and localizes to the cytosol, including confirmation in Arabidopsis roots. 3. Integration of metabolite fractionation, expression, and localization data identifies CsTSI as the key root L-theanine synthase.
